original_abstract:
Integrating sex and gender dimensions into biomedical research is essential for enhancing clinical accuracy, optimizing health outcomes, and mitigating therapeutic risks. Historically, scientific inquiry has often neglected these variables, leading to critical knowledge gaps. This study evaluates the efficacy of sex/gender-specific funding policies and journal editorial standards in increasing the integration of these perspectives within biomedical literature. We conducted a cross-national bibliometric and policy analysis covering 2000–2021, focusing on the United States, Canada, the United Kingdom, Japan, China, and South Korea. Using data from Dimensions AI, we employed interrupted time-series (ITS) analysis and linear regression to track shifts in research grants and publication outputs. 

Our results demonstrate that the implementation of formal mandates—such as the NIH’s "Sex as a Biological Variable" (SABV) policy in the U.S. and CIHR’s "Gender-Based Analysis Plus" (GBA+) in Canada—has led to significant increases in both targeted funding and research performance. Conversely, countries without such national mandates showed lower rates of integration. Furthermore, the adoption of sex/gender reporting standards by high-impact journals (e.g., Nature, Cell, and The Lancet) starting around 2017 served as a global catalyst, significantly increasing research quality and output even in regions lacking dedicated funding policies. While disciplines like psychology showed high levels of integration, health sciences exhibited more varied results, suggesting a need for more targeted translation of funding into published outcomes. These findings emphasize that a combination of top-down funding requirements and gatekeeping by prestigious journals is vital to standardizing sex and gender analysis, ultimately fostering more equitable and robust scientific advancements.
humanized_abstract:
See Related Articles Bringing sex and gender analyses to biomedical research will improve clinical validity, efficacy and safety of interventions and health outcomes. However, these analyses have often been overlooked. Our study investigated the effect of sex/gender-specific funding allocations to improve the representation of sex and gender analyses in biomedical literature. Using a mixed-methods approach combining bibliometric and policy analysis at the country level for the US, Canada, UK, Japan, China, and South Korea from 2000 to 2021, we examined, using Dimensions AI, using an interrupted time-series (ITS) analysis and linear regression models, the change in research funding and scholarly publications over time.

The number of countries with formal mandates increased while the number of countries with the appropriate funding to support research, and consequently better research outcomes, rose from 10 to 22 (from 23% to 38%) following the implementation of national sex/gender mandates, such as the NIH’s SABV policy in the US and the CIHR’s GBA+ policy in Canada. The number of countries with publications that comply with the Fossil Guideline also increased from 14 to 23 (from 21% to 36%) internationally after leading journals like Nature, Cell, and The Lancet implemented the guidelines starting around 2017. The inclusion of psychology within the biology framework has resulted in good progress and the health sciences appear to need more efficient translation of funds into high quality research output and publications. Our results highlight the importance of implementing formal mandates in conjunction with the support of top tier journals in advancing on the inclusion of sex/gender analysis in global research in order to achieve fair and quality science for women and girls.
